text:
THORACIC ONCOLOGY -- FOLLOW-UP VISIT
Department of Medical Oncology / Thoracic Oncology Program

ATTENDING ONCOLOGIST: [Attending Physician]
FELLOW: [Fellow Physician]

---

PATIENT: Margaret Chen
DOB: [on file]
MRN: [on file]
Diagnosis: Stage IIIB (T3N3M0) non-small cell lung carcinoma, adenocarcinoma, left upper lobe; EGFR L858R exon 21 activating mutation; TP53 co-mutation; PD-L1 TPS 15%
Current Regimen: osi (Tagrisso) 80 mg orally once daily
Line of Therapy: First-line
Cycle/Duration: Approximately 9 months of continuous therapy

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT / REASON FOR VISIT:

Ms. Chen presents for her scheduled 9-month oncology follow-up visit while receiving Tagrisso for her EGFR-mutant stage IIIB lung adenocarcinoma. She comes in today for review of recent restaging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis and surveillance MRI brain, as well as management of ongoing treatment-related skin toxicities. She reports overall feeling reasonably well and remains active at home.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:

Ms. Chen is a 63-year-old female (now 63 years of age) with a history of stage IIIB (T3N3M0) left upper lobe lung adenocarcinoma harboring an EGFR L858R activating mutation (exon 21), TP53 p.R175H co-mutation, and CDKN2A deletion. She was initially staged with a 4.2 cm spiculated left upper lobe primary mass, mediastinal lymphadenopathy at stations 4L, 5, and 6, and left supraclavicular lymphadenopathy on CT and PET-CT. MRI brain at diagnosis was negative for intracranial disease. Diagnosis was established by CT-guided core needle biopsy showing acinar-predominant invasive adenocarcinoma, TTF-1 and Napsin-A positive. NGS at the time of diagnosis confirmed the EGFR L858R mutation (allele frequency 38%) with no T790M resistance mutation, and low TMB (4 mut/Mb), MSS. PD-L1 TPS was 15%.

Following multidisciplinary tumor board review, given N3 disease (ipsilateral supraclavicular lymphadenopathy) and the presence of a sensitizing EGFR mutation, a decision was made to initiate first-line osi 80 mg daily as the primary systemic therapy, with radiation oncology evaluation for potential subsequent consolidative thoracic radiotherapy under active discussion. She started osi approximately 9 months ago.

At her 3-month response assessment, CT chest/abdomen/pelvis demonstrated an excellent partial response with the primary left upper lobe mass reducing from 4.2 cm to 2.1 cm (approximately 50% reduction), with decreased mediastinal adenopathy and near-complete resolution of the supraclavicular lymphadenopathy. QTc at that time was 432 ms. She was seen in follow-up at 3 months and was continued on full-dose Tagrisso.

She now returns for her 9-month assessment. She completed her restaging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis and surveillance MRI brain earlier this week, and results are available for review today.

With respect to disease-related symptoms, she denies any new cough, hemoptysis, worsening dyspnea, or chest pain. She has no shoulder pain, bone pain, or new neurological symptoms such as headaches, visual changes, focal weakness, or gait difficulty. She reports a mild, persistent dry cough that has been stable and which she attributes to residual effects of her prior mediastinal disease and possibly to osi. She has no dysphagia or odynophagia.

With respect to treatment-related toxicities: She developed grade 2 acneiform rash over the face and upper chest and grade 1 diarrhea early in her treatment course (approximately 4 weeks after starting Tagrisso). The rash was managed with topical clindamycin and a course of doxycycline 100 mg daily; diarrhea was managed with PRN loperamide. At today's visit, she notes the acneiform rash has significantly improved and is now much milder -- occasional papules on the nose and nasolateral folds, not bothersome, with minimal impact on daily activities. She is still applying topical clindamycin intermittently. Diarrhea has been minimal (1 loose stool every few days at most) and she rarely uses loperamide now.

Of new concern, she has noticed some tenderness and redness at the lateral nail fold of her left index finger over the past several weeks. She has been soaking it in warm water but would like guidance on further management. She does not report similar issues with other fingers or toes at this time.

She denies palpitations, near-syncope, dyspnea on exertion beyond her chronic mild baseline, or lower extremity edema. No recent illness or infections. No changes to her other medications.

She completed about 6 months of doxycycline for the rash before it was discontinued; she is not currently taking prophylactic antibiotics.

Overall, she rates her quality of life as 7/10 and feels the treatment is manageable. She remains active at home, doing light housework and taking daily walks of 20-30 minutes with her husband.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS:

- Constitutional: Mild fatigue (baseline), no fever, no night sweats, no significant unintentional weight loss (reports stable weight, down approximately 1 lb from last visit)
- HEENT: No headaches, no vision changes, no diplopia
- Respiratory: Mild dry cough (stable), no hemoptysis, no progressive dyspnea at rest; reports mild dyspnea with moderate exertion (stable)
- Cardiovascular: No chest pain, no palpitations, no lower extremity edema
- Gastrointestinal: Occasional loose stools (1 per several days), no significant diarrhea, no nausea, no vomiting, no abdominal pain, no melena
- Neurological: No headaches, no focal weakness, no sensory changes, no ataxia, no cognitive complaints
- Dermatologic: Mild residual acneiform papules on face (improving), grade 1 paronychia left index finger (new)
- Musculoskeletal: No new bone pain or joint pain
- Endocrine: No polyuria, no polydipsia (diabetes well-controlled per patient report)
- Psychiatric: Mild anxiety (well-managed), no depression
- Infectious: No fevers, no chills, no URI or GI illness

---

MEDICATIONS (current):

1. osi (Tagrisso) 80 mg orally once daily -- EGFR TKI, first-line therapy
2. Lisinopril 10 mg orally once daily -- hypertension
3. Amlodipine 5 mg orally once daily -- hypertension
4. Metformin 1000 mg orally twice daily -- type 2 diabetes mellitus
5. Atorvastatin 40 mg orally once daily -- hyperlipidemia (per prior records)
6. Topical clindamycin 1% gel -- applied to affected skin areas PRN for acneiform rash
7. Loperamide 2 mg orally PRN -- for diarrhea (infrequent use)
8. Aspirin 81 mg orally once daily
9. Multivitamin once daily

ALLERGIES: NKDA (No Known Drug Allergies)

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:

Vitals:
- Blood Pressure: 128/76 mmHg
- Heart Rate: 72 bpm, regular
- Respiratory Rate: 16 breaths/min
- SpO2: 96% on room air
- Temperature: 36.8 degrees Celsius
- Weight: 64.2 kg (stable)
- Height: 163 cm
- BSA: 1.68 m2
- ECOG Performance Status: 1

General: Well-appearing female in no acute distress. Alert and oriented x3. Appears her stated age. In good spirits.

HEENT: Normocephalic, atraumatic. Sclerae anicteric. Pupils equal and reactive. Mild acneiform papules present on the nasolabial folds and nose -- scattered, fewer than 10 visible lesions, no pustules, no crusting. No facial edema. No periorbital puffiness. Oral mucosa moist, no aphthae, no mucositis.

Neck: Supple. No palpable cervical lymphadenopathy. No palpable supraclavicular lymphadenopathy bilaterally -- previously left supraclavicular adenopathy was prominent; now non-palpable, consistent with near-complete response. No thyromegaly.

Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. S1 and S2 normal. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops. No jugular venous distension. Peripheral pulses 2+ bilaterally.

Respiratory: Mild decreased breath sounds at the left upper lobe region. No wheezing. No crackles or rhonchi. Respiratory effort non-labored. No dullness to percussion.

Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. No hepatosplenomegaly appreciated. Bowel sounds present and normoactive.

Extremities: No clubbing, no cyanosis, no lower extremity edema. Nails examined: Left index finger -- lateral nail fold with mild erythema, mild tenderness to palpation, slight periungual swelling consistent with grade 1 paronychia. No purulence. No fluctuance. No other digits affected. Right hand and bilateral feet nails appear normal.

Neurological: Alert and fully oriented. Cranial nerves II-XII grossly intact. No focal motor deficits. Gait steady. Coordination intact. No pronator drift.

Skin (chest/back): Scattered acneiform papules on upper anterior chest; fewer than at prior visits. No pustules. No significant erythema. Improving compared with prior exam.

---

DIAGNOSTIC RESULTS:

Laboratory (drawn this visit):
- CBC: WBC 6.2 x 10^9/L (normal differential), Hgb 11.8 g/dL (mild anemia, stable), Plt 214 x 10^9/L
- CMP: Na 138, K 4.1, Cl 102, CO2 24, BUN 14, Cr 0.82, Glucose 102 mg/dL (fasting)
- LFTs: ALT 22 U/L, AST 19 U/L, Alk Phos 78 U/L, Total Bili 0.7 mg/dL -- all within normal limits
- Albumin 3.9 g/dL
- HbA1c: 7.1% (diabetes adequately controlled)
- Magnesium: 1.9 mEq/L (low-normal)
- QTc on ECG (today): 436 ms -- within acceptable range; no significant prolongation

Imaging -- CT Chest/Abdomen/Pelvis with Contrast (restaging, reviewed today):
Significant interval further decrease in the left upper lobe primary mass, now measuring approximately 1.5 cm in greatest dimension, down from 2.1 cm at the 3-month scan and from 4.2 cm at baseline -- representing a cumulative 64% reduction from the pretreatment measurement. The lesion retains a small residual spiculated appearance but is markedly smaller. Minimal residual mediastinal adenopathy remains, with no dominant pathologically enlarged nodes at stations 4L, 5, or 6 on this assessment. No left supraclavicular lymphadenopathy is appreciated. No new pulmonary nodules or masses. No pleural effusion. No new hepatic or adrenal lesions. No suspicious osseous lesions. Overall, an ongoing excellent partial response, with continued interval improvement compared with the 3-month assessment.

Imaging -- MRI Brain with and without Gadolinium (surveillance, reviewed today):
No evidence of intracranial metastatic disease. No new enhancing lesions. No leptomeningeal enhancement. Ventricles and sulci appear normal for age. White matter signal changes are minimal and nonspecific. This is a normal study without evidence of brain metastases. Reassuring intracranial surveillance.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN:

Ms. Chen is a 63-year-old female with stage IIIB (T3N3M0, now functionally stage IV-equivalent given N3 nodal disease and clinical trajectory) EGFR L858R-mutant left upper lobe lung adenocarcinoma, currently 9 months into first-line osi (Tagrisso) 80 mg daily therapy.

1. DISEASE STATUS -- CONTINUED PARTIAL RESPONSE:
Today's restaging CT demonstrates ongoing excellent response to osi, with the left upper lobe primary mass now measuring 1.5 cm, a 64% reduction from the pretreatment baseline of 4.2 cm. This represents continued interval improvement from the 3-month scan (2.1 cm). Mediastinal lymphadenopathy has nearly resolved, and there is no supraclavicular adenopathy -- previously one of the most prominent disease sites. Surveillance MRI brain today is negative, which is particularly reassuring given the known propensity for CNS spread in EGFR-mutant NSCLC. There is no evidence of new systemic metastatic disease.

This is an outstanding response to osi and the patient continues on full-dose 80 mg daily without modification. The depth and durability of this response are consistent with outcomes reported for EGFR L858R-mutant disease on Tagrisso. We will continue to monitor closely given that TP53 co-mutation and CDKN2A deletion in the baseline NGS carry some association with relatively shorter duration of response in prior studies, though this patient is doing very well at 9 months.

Plan: Continue Tagrisso 80 mg orally once daily. No dose modifications at this time. Next restaging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis in approximately 3 months (at the 12-month mark). MRI brain to be repeated at that time per surveillance schedule given the high-risk CNS biology of her disease.

2. EGFR TKI SKIN TOXICITY -- ACNEIFORM RASH, IMPROVING:
The acneiform rash, which was grade 2 early in treatment and required topical clindamycin and a course of oral doxycycline, has improved significantly and is now grade 1 at most. She has scattered papules confined to the nasolabial folds and nose, no pustules, and reports minimal discomfort. She is managing well with intermittent topical clindamycin 1% gel.

Plan: Continue topical clindamycin PRN. No need to restart oral doxycycline at this time. Counsel on continued photoprotection (broad-spectrum SPF 30+ sunscreen, avoidance of midday sun), gentle non-comedogenic facial moisturizer, and avoidance of harsh cleansers or exfoliants. Reinforce that rash improvement over time is common on TKI therapy. If rash worsens back to grade 2 or above, will consider reintroducing low-dose oral doxycycline.

3. PARONYCHIA -- LEFT INDEX FINGER, GRADE 1 (NEW):
New grade 1 paronychia of the left index finger lateral nail fold. This is a recognized class-effect toxicity of EGFR inhibitor therapy, occurring in a meaningful proportion of patients on prolonged TKI treatment. On examination, there is mild erythema and periungual swelling without fluctuance or purulence, consistent with grade 1 disease.

Plan: Advised warm water soaks 2-3 times daily for 10-15 minutes (with or without dilute white vinegar solution). Topical antibiotics: betaine/mupirocin 2% ointment applied to the affected nail fold twice daily. Protective measures: wear gloves for wet work or gardening; avoid trauma to the nail. Will monitor at next visit. If the paronychia progresses to grade 2 (significant pain, functional impairment) or develops signs of bacterial superinfection, will initiate oral antibiotics (doxycycline or cephalexin depending on clinical picture) and consider referral to dermatology. No dose reduction indicated at grade 1.

4. DIARRHEA:
Mild, largely resolved. Intermittent loose stools only. No significant functional impairment. No weight loss associated.

Plan: Continue PRN loperamide as needed. Reinforce dietary adjustments (low-residue diet as tolerated, adequate hydration). No intervention required at this time.

5. MILD ANEMIA:
Hgb 11.8 g/dL, mild, stable. Likely multifactorial (chronic disease, possible nutritional component). Not symptomatic at this time.

Plan: Recheck CBC at next visit. Iron studies and B12/folate to be checked if persistent or worsening. No transfusion or erythropoietin-stimulating agent indicated at this time.

6. DIABETES MANAGEMENT:
HbA1c 7.1% -- diabetes remains adequately controlled on current metformin regimen. Fasting glucose today 102 mg/dL. Not on PI3K inhibitor, so glucose monitoring will remain per standard of care.

Plan: Continue metformin 1000 mg BID. Follow up with primary care for ongoing diabetes management.

7. CARDIOVASCULAR / QTc MONITORING:
QTc 436 ms today, in an acceptable range. osi carries a class warning for QTc prolongation; monitoring every 3 months has been incorporated into follow-up schedule. Magnesium 1.9 mEq/L (borderline low-normal); recommend magnesium-rich dietary intake and consider supplemental magnesium oxide 400 mg daily.

Plan: Continue QTc monitoring on serial ECG with each follow-up visit. Oral magnesium oxide 400 mg daily recommended.

8. THORACIC RADIATION ONCOLOGY CONSULTATION:
At the time of tumor board discussion, radiation oncology evaluation for consolidative thoracic radiotherapy was raised as a potential future option given her initial stage IIIB presentation. Given the outstanding systemic response to osi with near-resolution of mediastinal disease, this discussion remains deferred for now. Will revisit at a multidisciplinary level if the patient achieves maximum imaging response and consolidative intent becomes appropriate.

Plan: Continue to defer thoracic radiation. Discuss at next multidisciplinary conference with Radiation Oncology as appropriate.

9. SUPPORTIVE CARE / QUALITY OF LIFE:
Patient is maintaining ECOG PS 1. She is active at home, walking daily with her husband. Mild fatigue is present but not limiting. Appetite is adequate; weight is stable.

Plan: Encourage continued light aerobic activity. Nutritional support as needed. Psychosocial support available through the oncology social work program if anxiety worsens. Patient engaged and adherent with her oral therapy -- reinforce importance of daily dosing and prompt reporting of new symptoms.

---

FOLLOW-UP:
- Next oncology visit: Approximately 3 months
- Labs at next visit: CBC, CMP, LFTs, magnesium, HbA1c
- Restaging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast in approximately 3 months (12-month on-treatment scan)
- MRI brain with and without gadolinium in approximately 3 months
- ECG for QTc assessment at next visit
- Patient instructed to call oncology nurse line for: new or worsening neurological symptoms (headache, weakness, visual changes), significant rash flare, inability to take oral medications, febrile illness, or any new/concerning symptoms

Time spent in this visit: 45 minutes (greater than 50% of time devoted to counseling and care coordination).

Electronically signed,
[Attending Physician, MD]
Thoracic Oncology
[Institution]
